So I think it's best if we just take our time and go through as much as we can, but not force it and go too fast. So anyways, kind of like starting out like college, the question is what is Story? And the real nitpicks thing is that everything is Story. And so even when the ad says Peach is 95 cents and doesn't, it's a Story, but we don't really consider that Story. We usually consider a Story to be a progression, something with a beginning and an end, and if we're lucky, a goal. And we're going to get into some really good stuff. But we really want to find out what makes a Story interesting and what also makes us care. So we're going to get into that in a minute because that's really what's important as you'll see. Okay, so the thing is I want to say that StoryTelling does many wonderful things. It helps us understand, helps us know, like, and trust total strangers. This is an important part. And it's a very powerful way to sell. It's used to sell politics, religion, and even cereal. And I want to show you guys a couple things. You guys can all see my camera, right? He went to jail and he got his life back together. But that's his Story. And it has nothing to do with the bread. But the know-like-it-trust comes from these types of stories. And here's another thing that we look at using Story to sell, which is our Trader Joe's, right? And here's a very important thing to know about this. This is paid to be printed and mailed. The whole story is that we're over the moon, under the moon, and anywhere else that gets us near the moon. Hopefully this doesn't give you any trouble. Okay, well let's go with the headline first. You guys need to remember this stuff. Keep it in your head a little bit. I'll do some reminders. It's the amazing diet secret of a desperate housewife. And if you haven't guessed already, this is a story. Okay, now, it's a story-based ad. Okay, so here we go. My name is Nancy Pryor. I am 35 years old. I'm a housewife and a mother of three children. I would like to tell you something personal about myself. One day last summer, I just broke down and started to cry. I couldn't help it. I had reached my breaking point. I felt like I was going to have a nervous breakdown. It happened one Monday morning, right after I got on the bathroom scales to weigh myself. I had been on a strict diet for nearly two months. I had gone off the diet over the weekend to give myself a break and just to live like a normal human being for a couple of days. Now the scales said I weighed 188 pounds. I just couldn't believe it. That meant that in one lousy weekend, I had more than gained back every single pound I had lost in the last three weeks. Maybe this doesn't sound like a big deal to you, but it felt like a tragedy. I had been trying to lose weight for the last five and a half years. I had tried liquid protein. I had tried fasting. I had tried hypnosis. I had tried exercise. I had tried sauna belts. For a while, I even used dangerous diet drugs. As far as diets go, I tried almost everyone I heard about. The Adkins diet, the Stillman diet, the Grapefruit diet, the Winnensky team diet, etc., etc. You name it, I tried it. The results were always the same. I would struggle and struggle to lose a few pounds. And then the very first time, I would give myself a small break. The weight would come right back on. It had happened many times before, but somehow this time it seemed like the last straw. I didn't know where to turn or what else to try. I was about to give up. Don't get me wrong, I don't want this to sound like a sob story. I'm not telling you all this because I want you to feel sorry for me. There is no need for that anyway. My story has a very happy ending. My story has a happy ending because the very next day, I made a discovery that changed my entire life. I found an amazing way to lose weight that I never dreamed existed. I'm not going to tell you where I found the secret. However, I will tell you this much. I did not get this information from a doctor. I did not get this information from a diet book. I did not find this information in a magazine. I did not find this information in a newspaper. I did not get this information from any of the so-called diet experts. To tell the truth, I stumbled across this information, totally by accident when I was looking for something else. But all that doesn't matter. What does matter is that last I have found an answer. Let me tell you why I think my diet is so fantastic. The nice thing is, we should do an ad breakdown of this, but the nice thing is now it gets out of the story. The story was just a primer. Here is a poll quote that's important to remember too. I have lost 75 pounds. My friends say I look 15 years younger. I enjoy a peace of mind I have never before experienced. I feel good all the time. Best of all, my husband has fallen in love with me all over again. We'll just get this last paragraph. Sparkling new body. This diet has been a godsend to me. I have a sparkling new body. I have lost 75 pounds. I now weigh 113 and wear a size 6. I used to wear an 18. I have more energy now than I had when I was a teenager. I am in better health than I can ever remember. My friends say I look 15 years younger. I enjoy a peace of mind I have never before experienced. I feel good all the time. Best of all, my husband has fallen in love with me all over again. Will this amazing little diet work for you? I am sure it will. All I ask is that you be open minded enough to give me a chance to prove it, even though I am an everyday housewife and not a diet doctor or anything like that. I have written a book that tells you about my secret diet. It is easy to read. It tells you exactly what to do step by step. You can read my book in one evening. Then the very next day you can start losing weight so fast you just won't believe it. End of story. Some of you out there probably know that is pretty amazing copy. Other people just think it is a regular story. Before there are books we read each other, we still do. Every minute of every day we instinctively know everyone has an agenda. We want to be sure that that agenda isn't to clobber us metaphorically or with a hammer. What we are hoping for is kindness, empathy, and maybe a nice box of chocolates. Yes, we definitely did that on the court. Okay, so a recent study with subjects underwent functional magnetic resonance image of MRI of the brain while reading a short story revealed that the areas of the brain that lit up when they read about an activity were identical to those that light up when they actually experienced it. Details about actions and sensation are captured from the text and integrated with personal knowledge from past experiences. These data are then run through mental simulations using brain regions that closely mirror those involved when people perform, imagine, or observe similar real world activities. In short, when we read a story we really do slip into the protagonist's skin feeling what she feels, experiencing what she experiences. So this is very powerful that in making people feel what the protagonist is feeling. As we talk about Maslow, everything, all the decisions. Remember we talked about the guy that had the brain damage? Actually it was actually in this book, I didn't quote it here, it's actually in this book about the guy who couldn't make a decision because he lost his emotions. He hadn't lost his ability to function like a calculator, he had lost his bunch to feel and feelings were all decision makers. So I want to make a note that you should always remember to get in parallel with your prospect's desire so you can write the story that is right in their wheelhouse so they can't help but see how their own story turns out. Because this is their story, remember? In the copyright thing we're talking about, their story is their story, your story is their story. So it makes it that this is a done deal, that all they have to do is buy the thing that has already been proven to work for their identical twin in the story. I hope that makes sense, it doesn't make sense to people, just say something. Okay, I don't know why I got an extra slide. Okay, the back door that bypasses the reader's natural skepticism. The more you have a message you want to convey, the more you have to trust your story to do it. The joy of reading is getting to make up your own mind about what a story's ultimate message is. The joy of writing is being stealthy enough to stack the deck so your reader will choose yours. Now this isn't exactly what my dad's doing but it's an interesting way because you get to the points in horror movies and stuff like that where they don't tell you what's going to happen, they give you clues of what's going to happen but you have no choice but know the guy's in the closet with the axe. I think it's a great thing to have people, if you stack the deck in such a way that Gary did stack it in his own ways, we'll get into that. But he stacked the deck in his own ways because he said, I tried this, I tried that, I tried this, I tried that. So he left you with no other exit. Exercise exits, no diet exits, no nothing. Anyways, and the other point I wanted to make on my notes is how can you hate someone who's suffered? How can you feel guarded against their suffering? You can't deny their story, it's theirs. So there's no resistance to someone else's story. Good writing versus storytelling. This is, we've heard Gary Howard talk about this, he's talked about the message like the person with bad English comes to tell you in the worst language. Possibly your wife has had triplets. The story is far more important than the way it's told. But it's also important. So there's a big difference between a good writer and a storyteller. Some of the best stories you can't remember, a single sentence of, except for someone like Shakespeare. But he was a good storyteller also. I remember when I was young, a lot of people didn't like the Leonardo DiCaprio version of Romeo and Juliet. Because they couldn't get past the poetic dialogue, they got lost in it. And so they lost the whole story, they lost all these people I knew that watched that version of that movie or tried to and bailed out of it. Can you imagine if you do that same thing in a copy? It's not the same. And so what also happens is when you do that stuff, when you get artful, when you get poetic, when you use a big word and all that stuff. What you do is you take the person out of the story and they have a background dialogue. What does that word mean? I have to sit and stop and use some thought process to figure it out. I've got to study the context. Few notions are more damaging to writers than the popular belief that writing a successful story is a matter of learning to write well. Who could argue that? It sounds so logical, so obvious. What would the alternative be, learning to write poorly? Okay, so I have a friend, he's an editor, I think he's a writer that I play volleyball with and he's kind of a little bit of a somewhat of a snob. And people are always talking about how bad the writers are that wrote the series of Twilight and wrote the series of Fifty Shades of Grey. They don't get the idea that nobody remembers what the paragraphs were. No one remembers what the sentences were. No one remembers what the words were, but they do remember being sucked into that story. And so there is a big difference between that being poetic. And so I ask you how many lines of your favorite stories do you remember? You remember some stories and not the words. So keep that in mind. Okay, here's her continuing. So why is it that they can venture code one of the best-selling novels of all time? Because from the very first page, readers are dying to know what happens next. And that's what matters most. A story must have the ability to engender a sense of urgency. From the first sentence, everything else, backwards characters, great dialogue, vivid imagery, luscious language is grainy. Make no mistake, learning to write well is not synonymous with learning to write a story. And of the two, writing well secondary. Because if the reader doesn't know what happens next, so what if it's well-written? In the trade, such exquisitely rendered, story-less novels are often referred to as beautifully written, who cares? Bonfraub, I love that one now. Which gets into this. It's not in the sense, so what? Elmer Leonard famously said, that a story is real life with the boring parts left out. Not looking for a reader to change, sell them the same. That's me, this is her. The good news is that everyone, real fictional or somewhere in between has a goal. Even those who want to remain exactly as they are and never change, and I owe to have a goal. In fact, it's the biggest challenge of all, staying the same in the face of the constant onslaught of perpetual change is no easy task. Okay, so one of the things I wanted to point here was, you know what I'm saying about story, and it was the stupid thing. I was like, I need to think of leather stories of ways to help people sell in some of the drier markets or whatever you want to call it. And I was thinking of insurance, and I was like, well, that's not a dry area to talk about. And I started, you know, it's interesting when the light switch on and you can't stop thinking about it. And I was like, there's two things that remind me. One is that what is the downside of the story of a guy who doesn't get his homeowner's insurance? I mean, the downside is, now you could say he loses his house, he loses his money, but you want to make it emotional. His wife has to do this, his kids have to move away from home into a new zip code and lose all their friends and all this other stuff and all the years that they set back. So, yeah, there's always a downside, or an upside, that you can tell. And one of the interesting things about the history of this, I think it was in Japan, they were selling life insurance, and they did something very clever. When they were selling in life insurance, do you know who they used to have them go out and sell life insurance door to door? They had widows go do it. They had the widows, yes. So there they were staring at the story, staring at the story of what they don't want to see happen to them. So, that was very interesting. Okay, so let me just see. I'm just my notes here. People will fight harder to keep from losing something than gain it in the first place. And we've heard, I've read about this many a time, you know, there's a guy, you can't sell him a set of tires, but if you tell him someone's down at the tire shop about to steal him, he'll get out of bed and race to go get him. Okay, I've got all those notes covered. All right, headline. John Irving once said, whenever possible, tell the whole story of the novel in the first sentence. And being lean and efficient, he pretty much did that, didn't he? He pretty much told you. I mean, he didn't say amazing tragic diet secret, but I mean, you really got the idea that we were getting, that there was a great ending. And we talked about the Craig Clemens thing about how he went down a dark alley, but he pulled himself out of it from the don't get me wrong, I don't want this to sound like a sob story. I'm not telling you this because I want you to feel sorry for me. There's no need for that. My story is a very happy ending, which I thought was great because that's a way to really go down the dark alley. If you make sure you time the moment you give him a little relief from the negative energy. Let's see. Yes, so sometimes I have to tell the happy ending. So people won't want the ending, but rather the story of how you got there. I used to suffer from X. Here's how I achieved X in Japan and why I think you can do it in America too. This is from Thomas Hall, Direct Mail Guys. And it's also in the Direct Mail, the amazing diet secret of a desperate housewife. All right, power story. Do we know whose story it is? This is her. This is me. Okay. Body copy. This is interesting because it's the segment she's talking to. Dovetail right into the components of an ad. Okay, there must be someone through whose eyes we're viewing the world we've been plunked into, AKA the protagonist. Think of your protagonist as the reader serving it. In the world that you, the writer, are creating, is something happening beginning on the first page. Don't just set the stage for later conflict. Jump right in with something that will affect the protagonist and so make the reader hungry to find out what the consequence will be. After all, unless something is already happening, how can we want to know what happens next? Is there conflict? What's happening? Will the conflict have a direct impact on the protagonist's quest, even though your reader might not yet know what that quest is? Is something at stake on the first page? And as important is your reader of where, of what it is. Okay. So are you guys getting the goal that's in this book? Ten bucks, you can get this goal. All right. So, I mean... Kevin, I've already written it down like, okay, and I'm going to go to Amazon and pick this puppy up right away. Yes, absolutely. Well, do you have professional copyright? Like David Garfinkel saying for 20 years and you've been around for 30 years wondering how this is just priceless. Yes, I'm glad you guys enjoy it. All right. I felt like I was going to have a nervous breakdown. Can we put any more tension in this thing? Could Gary Halbert put any one more on this? Is he doing just what she said here in the body copy right there, right in front of your face? Okay. Let's see. What do I have on my notes there? Another one. They all laughed when I sat at the piano. Remember that one? Okay. Right. So, storytelling. Who are you and why should I listen to your answer? I was suffering just like you until I discovered this. Will this desperate housewife find a solution? I just read what I read to you. Oh, and the interesting thing was is that when it came to the very first sentence of the body copy, I will say this has to come first. This is important. Who she is has to come first because it will nag the reader and they will be fully immersed in your story without it. They'll spend their time. Whereas yet they will start searching around to find out who the story is about. And in that time they may throw the hook, but the headline set, they may dissolve interest. Okay. So, it's kind of important thing. All right. That's me by the way. All right. Next. All right. Myth. The plot is what the story is about. Reality, a story is about how the plot affects the protagonist while thus far it's been implied. It helps to say it flat out. Plot is not synonymous with story. Plot facilitates story by forcing the protagonist to confront and deal with the issue that keeps him from achieving his goal. The way the world treats him, how he reacts, reveals his name. Okay. So, what do I have? Do I have a note here? Yes. So, what is the plot of our story? It's that nothing she's tried has worked. I've been trying to lose weight for five and a half years. I've tried liquid protein. I've tried fasting. I've tried hypnosis. I've tried exercise. I've tried sauna belts. For a while I even used dangerous diet drugs. As far as diets go, I tried almost every one I heard about. Yeah, I can diet stone at night. You get it? Okay. And I wrote, I tried it. This is where Gary lists all the diets she tried and her struggle with her hunger and willpower. All right. What's the state? What hangs in the bounce? Where's the conflict? Conflict is stories like what? Another seeming no-brainer. But there's a bit of helpful fine print that often goes unwritten. We're not talking about just any conflict, but conflict that is specific to the protagonist's quest. From the first sentence, readers morph into blood hounds, relentlessly trying to sniff out what is the stake here and how will it impact the protagonist. Okay. So hit the ground running. Be immersive. What's the stake in this ad? I think, Basil, we already talked about this. Okay. Tone. We're talking about tone or voice, right? It's like tone of voice, which often says more than the words themselves. In fact, sometimes tone says the exact opposite of what the words are saying. Does anyone who's ever been in a long-term relationship can attest? I thought I was going to cut this out. Your stories' tones reflect how you see your characters and help define the world. You set the most tone as often as you can. You guys hear that? Tone is often how a theme is conveyed by killing your readers into the emotional prism through which you want them to view your story like a soundtrack in a movie. It's another way of sharpening your focus, highlighting what your reader really needs to know. So in this case, we're talking about slang and lingo and choice of words are prospects if we need it to be. I wrote here from this copy. That meant in one lousy week and I had more than gained back every single pound I had lost in the last three weeks. There's a whole bunch of more, but we're going to get into it. What we're going to notice is that everything about the person we're writing about, and I'm going to give it away early, is that we have a very humble person. That's one of the things that was also the genius of the Code of Arms of Letter. It was coming from not salespeople, it was coming from a humble person. Call to action. This is where her stuff fits in there. Here's a disconcerting thought. Marketers, politicians, and televangelists know more about story than most writers. This is because by definition, they start with something writers often never even think about. The point their story will make. Armed with that knowledge, they then craft a tale which every word, every image, every nuance leads directly to it. Okay. I'm going to go through it once in a while so you guys can see if you guys are waving at me on camera. Power store. Okay. Writers often have to stop and think about what it is they're trying to say and what point their story is making. It's crucial because the instant a reader opens your book, his cognitive unconscious is hunting for a way to make life a little easier. See things a little clearer, understand people a little bit better. So why not take a second to ask yourself, what is it I want my readers to walk away thinking about? What point does my story make? How do I want to change the way my reader sees the world? Okay. I hope everyone's just a law of her. Genius. Okay. Your avatar. So we're getting all the pieces of a copy. It's all in this thing about story and writing books and novels. Do you know what your protagonist's internal goal is? One way of arriving at this is to ask yourself, what does achieving her external goal mean to her? How does she think it will affect how she sees herself? What does she think it will say about her? Is she right? Or is her internal goal at odds with her external goal? We're going to get into what is her internal external goal. Does your protagonist's goal force her to face a specific long-standing problem or fear? What secret terror must she face to get there? What deeply helped belief will she have to question? What has she spent her whole life according that she must now either look at straight in the eye or wave the white flag of defeat? We'll get to that. Some of those pieces later. We're actually cruising through this pretty good. Oh my God. This is another quote, another later. I just thought it was important for... Look at it this way. A butterfly may be a beautiful in and of itself, but what makes it interesting is that it used to be a caterpillar. The before is the yardstick that allows the reader to measure the protagonist's progress toward after. Now, do I have to read you a piece of the ad again and show that Gary Howard did the caterpillar? Let's see. We know he did, right? And my computer is freezing up. All right. That's my sign to not torture you with... Oh, there we go. Who said... Let's see. I've lost 75 pounds. I now weigh 113 and wear a size six dress I used to wear in 18. That's a caterpillar if you ask me. Okay. So, move it on. What else has Lisa got for us? Power story. Asking them embarrassing questions about the more personal, the better. Seek out the good, the bad, and especially the ugly, the messy, and the secrets they'd really rather keep to themselves. Nothing can be off limits. Rather than overlook their flaws, you want to pinpoint each one and then examine it under a high-powered microscope in light of their internal issue and their goal. Your goal is to allow them to be full, complete, flesh-and-blood characters who, like us, are doing their best to muddle through against all odds. The essence of a story lies in revealing the things that in real life we don't say outlaw. This is why, as cool as it may feel, you can't allow your characters any privacy or mercy when exploring their past. So, one day last summer I just broke down and started to cry. I couldn't help it. I reached my breaking point. I felt like I was going to have a nervous breakdown. This is humanizing our character. We talked long about how Craig Clemens gets into some state where he has to sit and think for a while about who his real character is and just delve deep into the guy that's... I forget what he described his prospects as, but got into it pretty deep. Make it personal. The general versus the specific. In October 2006, nearly 6,000 people worldwide perished in hurricane-induced floods. Quick, what do you feel after reading that sentence? My guess is you feel a little confused by the question. Now, imagine a wall of water coming straight towards a small boy who cleansed desperately to his frantic mother, trying to soothe him. She whispers, don't worry baby, I'm here. I won't let you go. She feels a relaxing in the moment of deafening calm just before the water rips him from her arms. The sound of his cry above the cacophony of destruction, trees ripped from the ground, houses smashed his plenaries, will haunt her for the rest of her life. That and his look of utter surprise as he was swept away. I trust you, it seemed to say, and you let me go. Now, how do you feel? This time, the question is clear. Watching the flood claim that one little boy is far more gut-wrenching than hearing about 6,000 anonymous people perishing in various floods. Okay, so this is what James Karaman did in the movie Titanic. And I have a note here, story, make it personal. Don't just get to the satellite view of a city in the middle of a war. Get down to the street level and follow a mother as she and her daughter run for cover. Like the little girl in the red coat in Schindler's List. I don't know how many people saw that. Thinking versus feeling. This is, okay, 57 out of 63. The point is, if I ask you to think about something, you can decide not to. But if I make you feel something, now I have your attention. Feeling is a reaction. Our feelings let us know what matters to us. And our thoughts have no choice but to follow. Facts don't affect us either directly or because we can't imagine how the facts affect someone else. Don't matter to us, I guess. And that explains why one personalized story has infallible more impact than an impersonal generalization, even though the scope of the generalization is a thousand times greater. We'll have done it, but that's all right. Make them feel, okay, 59 out of 63. In the story, what the reader feels is driven by what the protagonist feels. Story is visceral. We climb inside the protagonist's skin and become sensei, feeling what he feels. Otherwise, we have no portimentary, no point of view through which to see, evaluate, and experience the world the author has plumped us into. This girl is great. This means that every... Let me see if I'm missing any notes. Yeah, I wanted to make a note here. I said, story is real life hypnosis. As you've seen through the MRI studies, everything. This is literally hypnosis. If you see a movie where someone's jumping out of the screen and going to kill somebody, everyone jumps out of the street, out of their seat, but there's no real threat, but we're that much in immersions. Okay. The power story. This means that everything in the story gets its emotional weight in a meaning based on how it affects the protagonist. If it doesn't affect her, even if we're talking birth, death, or the fall of the Roman Empire, it is completely neutral. And guess what? Neutrality bores the reader. If it's neutral, it's not only beside the point, it detracts from it. Okay. So I wrote here, can you see how this is a softer backdoor approach to selling? If we strategically box our protagonist in and make them face the only real solution he has, then we do the same to our reader. And then we say, I didn't know where else to turn. I didn't know what else to try. I was about to give up. That's a good book from that. All right. 61 and 63. When your protagonist's reaction is up close and personal, it catapults us into a skin where we become sensile, feeling what he feels. And there we remain throughout the entire story. This isn't to say we won't feel what other characters feel as well, but ultimately what other characters do think and feel will itself be measured. By its effect on the protagonist. So you can have a lot of antagonists. Okay. And 62. That's what readers come for. Their unspoken hand-wired question is, if something like this happens to me, what would it feel like? How should I best react? Okay. Last slide. Myth. Write what you know. Reality. Write what you know emotionally. If your protagonist is a trumpet-playing neurosurgeon turned CIA agent stationed in Antarctica, yes, you'd better know something about each of those things. But in the larger sense, write what you know. Doesn't refer to as much to facts as to what you know emotionally, which translates to your knowledge of what makes people tick. Writing what you actually know, however, is a dangerous game given our natural propensity to tacitly assume that others have the same knowledge and beliefs that we do. This tendency drives what communication scholars Chip and Dan Heath have done, the curse of knowledge. Didn't we talk about the curse of knowledge last week? I think we did. And it becomes difficult for us to share our knowledge with others because we can't readily create our listeners state of mind. And this was one of the things that we talked about that Gary Halbert was great at. Gary Halbert was very good at wiping his memory. He could hold up two pieces of memory. He could hold up the knowledge he needed to know about a client's thing he needed to sell. And he could raise his mind like a prospect and know how to explain it to a friend. Like a math problem you just got, but the teacher can't explain it to your friend. Just figure it out and can explain it to you and make that connection. So that's all I have for that. When it's at its best, it's just magic. I've got a question for you, Kevin. I hope I can answer it. Kevin, Lisa's not here. Well, okay. You guys know, you guys on the webinar, hopefully you remember. I'm actually a classically trained writer. I did the whole BFA and creative writing and all of that. And what she's talking about, of course, is delineated for someone who's writing a book. But it does translate perfectly. My question is this. One thing you were talking about, she was talking about self-same. But then she talks about how do I want to change the way my reader sees the world? Can you explain how you walk that very fine line? Well, I think they're two different animals or two different things. One is that some guy trying to win the Kentucky Derby is one thing. Sophie's choice is another thing. It's a powerful thing to not want to keep both your children. It's to keep from losing. But another one is what is my goal? What am I trying to do? Now, Sophie's choice, the goal is to make the right decision or not lose any kids. Her goal is different. Her goal is to resist change. But the other guy is to achieve. We'll put on that Maslow. I think it's self-actualization. It might be the one just below it. Does that answer your question? I don't know. I'm not the only one who can answer that. Yeah, Bon's got his hand up. Yeah. One is what you're talking about is sort of like a technique of persuasion that you can do. The way that the story helps this is you want to meet them where they are. You never win an argument going up against somebody's beliefs. The way that you win an argument is you demonstrate and prove to them that you fully understand and share their beliefs. I call it the Aikido method of taking their energy. I absolutely believe this is the horrible thing and everything else. But then there was this other aspect that I didn't think about. Then you start bringing them into where you want them to be and where their world is. That's when they're not thinking about it or they don't agree with you. The easiest way to do it is to make sure that you're going with that people where they will agree with you. One of the things that a lot of people think mistakenly because they say persuasion and marketing and everything is I hate this phrase that they always say, your dad could have sold ice to Eskimos. I was getting mad at that. I'm like, then you didn't pay attention to him because he would never try and sell ice to Eskimo. He would try and sell them heaters. It's hard enough to sell products that are good that people want that people can use. Don't fight it by trying to struggle with trying to change people's minds. Now, that being said, sometimes your marketing goal is to change people's minds about something. That's the tactics you need to use in that. I think one of the key things that briefly, and I know Kevin's covered this but sums up that's really important here, is the story basically starts with a situation all the time. I learned this in college that all stories have a, my teacher was pretty lame. He called it an epiphany, but the truth is all stories have just a change. Otherwise there's no story. Otherwise you're stating a fact. The car drove down the street. That's not a story. The car down the street and crashed. Okay, there's a change. That's the story. There's always the situation, whatever it's person, place, thing, or product, or environment, marketplace. There's the change and then there's the result afterwards. Now, you don't always have to give the result in creative writing, but in marketing you do. The most important part that Kevin was talking about is telling the situation emotionally beforehand. Describing it emotionally is very important. Then the change happens. That's the smallest part of the actual story. That's the, like in the 80s, the change was that montage of shots in all the movies where they spent so much time showing you how Ralph Marchet was getting his ass kicked and being bullied at school and all this. Then they show you the montage of paint the fence, the sand the floor, and all this other stuff. That was the change. It was the smallest part. Then they went into the results and the story and they spent more time on that. So in marketing though, especially, well, I've noticed with my dad's, the biggest portion is the beginning. That's where he spent most, always spent the time. In fact, there was one ad that we were talking about. He never even finished the story. It was the amazing secret of the marketing genius who was afraid to fly. He never told the secret. He never said, this is a guy's secret. So the point that I want to make is in marketing, learn to describe the emotions. That also means in your research, when you're looking up on, you know, that we talk about with your negative reviews and positive reviews and informs and stuff, pay attention to the emotional vocabulary they use. When they say, I'm frustrated, write that down. When they say, this product made me want to tear my eyes out because it was so bad. You want to kind of be able to mimic that and pay attention to where it is elsewhere. You know what I'm saying? But in any case, I hope that answers your question because I think what you have to do when you persuade people is just meet them where they are. You always have to meet them where they are and demonstrate that you fully understand their position. And only then could you guide them to a different way of thought. And it usually can't be straight back. It usually has to be in a way like, oh, I never really thought about it that way. You know, and go there. But for the most part, you know, in your research, you're shooting for people that are going to agree with everything you want and eagerly, you know, you're shooting to also reinforce their feelings. And this was a great ad, by the way, to describing how a, you know, someone, it's a great ad for the emotional description and how people feel. This is the biggest problem I have as a copywriter. I'm going to share my secret right now. The biggest problem I have is because I come from the education background that I come from. I try to write two dollar words all the time because that's my vocabulary. I, you know, it's like Garfinkel was saying last week, you know, copywriters, we're writers of all sorts. But copywriters too, we're voracious readers and it expands our vocabulary. And that is one of the biggest problems I have is that being able to write it in a way that emotionally grabs and yanks them, pulls them right down to the next line and the next line and the next line without making them stumble over one of those big two dollar words. Have a kid read it. Yeah. Have a kid read it. And Mark, have a kid read it. Yeah, I do actually. You know, that's one of the beauties about having a teenage daughter at home. Yeah. Oh, is this a Sramko or somebody has had like an even younger kid reading it and gave him like a quarter every time he found a word that was unpronounceable. But I'll give you a couple of tips I'm keeping them reading in that, in that idea, which is you want to, Kevin might talk about it with, you want to write complete sentences that have incomplete thoughts. So in other words, where you'll see where my dad hang stuff and leave stuff, you're still always wanting to know the next sentence. You know, one of the things that I always say is people are looking for a place to stop reading. You know, Craig was talking about the news and Facebook and everything. You're looking for a good place to say, okay, look, I've seen this other content before. I'm caught up with all my friends. I feel safe shutting this down and knowing that I'm on touch with that. When you're looking at a newspaper, you're going through the articles and going, okay, I'm going to read enough of this newspaper to feel fully informed of this article. And once I feel that I've got the main gist enough, I feel comfortable moving on. People are looking for a place to stop reading, to stop, you know, in getting themselves involved in the content. And I try not to give it to them until they are absolutely, until you feel their desire so much that, you know, they're just, they're the only reason they want to stop reading is to buy. And one of the ways that we do that is where we break up sentences. And it's not always leaving it on a cliffhanger like saying, but there's more. It's basically saying things like, you know, oh, I just got out of jail. Or it makes you go, okay, I can't leave it right there, right? Or somebody says, you know, this had three major effects, you know, which actually seemed minor to the big problem, okay? You're now hanging on there and you can't stop reading at that point. And one of the other things I want to mention, because you mentioned college, one of the things that you have to undo, I did that, my daughter was asking me for some writing advice because she's in high school. And I said, well, what kind of teacher do you have? Because there's two kinds of writing. There's good writing and then there's school writing. School writing teaches you to state a premise, leave three to five supporting arguments, and then sum it all up at the end. That's going to bore the crap out of anybody who doesn't have to read it. That's designed to disseminate information from manager to a CEO or to whatever. In school, they teach you to take a paragraph and expound on it for five pages, or take, you know, a full philosophy and try and boil it down into a paragraph. And that's just nonsense, okay, as far as for real world writing skill. When it comes to real world writing skill, what you want to do is understand who your prospect is, grip them like Craig was talking about, grip them right from the beginning, then and give them something really salacious that they can't stop reading, and then keep going with those incomplete kind of thoughts and, you know, with complete sentences. And to a point where they're now so wrapped in and they realize there's something for them at the end of this, that there's something for them that you're talking about that is really important to their lives, losing weight, you know, getting acceptance, you know, finding peace of mind, whatever it is, that there's this emotional word at it, and keep them going there. And that's the point where you can, you know, you start giving them the bullet points or you start giving them all of the details that are going to fuel their desire. And that's the only time I would stop using that technique. But you want to kind of, I have a degree in college, my dad was kind of lamented the fact that I got it. He was always very proud that he asked me like, how would they do? And I said, oh, I know more than my professors, and I did. I had more experience than all of them. And they knew it too. And some of them would even ask me questions. And the funny thing was, it really was true. You could learn more hanging out with my dad for a month than you could for a year in those writing courses for this kind of marketing, for the purpose of marketing. I'm not going to talk about anything else. But, yeah, you can't, you know, all the people think that they can't write copy because they didn't go to college or that they're worried about their language skills. It's the exact opposite. You've got to be able to dumb down your copy. And I don't mean it as an insult. I mean, you look for the smaller word. If I write something and go, oh, that's written really impressive. And I rounded out and stuff like that. I go back and erase it because I don't want to be clever. You can be clever with your hook. You can be clever a little bit with your play on words for your attention-getting device. But you're not, your copy and your story is not supposed to be clever. It's supposed to be compelling. And the way that you do that is really amping up and showing more pain. And the other thing I wanted to add was when you do tell the story, your negative part of the story, I was down and out, make it worse than the prospects, than the average prospect. You want them to say, this guy had it worse than me. Okay. Right on. So Mark, do you have anything you'd like to ask or add? We can't hear you. I think Kevin covered it pretty well. You know, the stories emanate from the days where we sat round a fire. And that's how we communicated our knowledge and passed it down. And a lot of religions, I spoke with that, I drew a priest a few years ago. I can't remember the number he said. I think it was something ridiculous, like 20,000 stories they had to memorize that had been passed down through hundreds of years. And I think that's why we intuitively want to listen to a story, is because of some part of our brain is always looking for that piece of knowledge that could help us, preserve us, or learn or something. So in that respect, I think there were all children when it comes to stories. I think Kevin covered it well. Yeah. Well, the sheet covers that. I only covered a tiny fraction of what this book covers, but she talks about that. And that story was vital. It was survival. It was survival through the experiences that someone else went. I was wondering about that. And I was thinking about it just today or yesterday. I was like, because we had our webinars, like, that was the one thing that got me lost. Why are we so curious that it sometimes kills us? And I was like, oh, no wonder. Because if we ignore that thing, we might ignore something, might jump out of the bush and kill us. So we have dying of curiosity to know what could be threatening us. What's that growling sound around that rock? Was it, you know, was it cautiously figured out? But yeah, she covers all that stuff in this book. It's amazing. And that's the thing. It's not like a writer's guide, blah, blah, blah. It's like why we are wired this way. We can't. Why are you having no choice in this stuff? Well, it's evolution, the truth, exactly what you're both saying, which is when we sat around the campfires, they told the story. If you look at the old fairy tales, the old songs and lyrics, they're all designed to warn you of something, you know, little red writing hood and stuff like that. And the thing is, the people who survived were the people who were able to remember the stories. They were able to remember the dangers. We are, evolution has weeded out the people who, you know, were like, you needed it in writing or you needed it in some other form of learning. So our brain is, when you say hardwired, you know, all that, it is literally a product of evolution. If stories didn't stick in our brains, they died. And so yeah, you know, that. And the other thing though that I also want to say is a benefit, which I think we've talked about, everybody gets, but I just want to state it emphatically, which is stories get remembered. Nobody remembers dates and numbers. Well, some people do, but you know, for the most part, people remember the stories. If you go back to, you know, I mean, I'm afraid, you know, he says, I have my memory shot and it's terrible, but he remembers every little story lyric because songs tell stories, you know, like Scooby-Doo's, the things, stories and, you know, stories are something that are, remind you so much. And here's where they're really, really important is when you need somebody to remember something for later. Because a lot of times, you know, we're speaking, we're specifically talking on direct response. So we want them to hit a button, take an action right now. But there are cases in marketing where you're talking to somebody on a TV and what they've got to buy is in the supermarket, right? And so what you need with that story and the whole point of the jingle is to make them remember, stick in their brain. They might not remember why this is better. You know, they might not remember your exact, you know, that this one's got, you know, 15% more of the active ingredient, but they will remember the story of something. And that's one of the things, it makes it even, it's always critical, but it makes it even more critical to further away somebody, the more you're expecting the prospect to have to remember your situation before they purchase.
